_comment: Cross-file refactor workload. Self-contained Python project where three services each have THREE duplicated validators (email, phone, url) that drifted into three variant implementations across services (9 duplicated function bodies total). Agent consolidates into a single canonical `src/validators.py`, wires all three services through it, keeps all 81 existing tests passing. Designed to exercise the multi-file edit + dedup task shape distinct from the gap-find-and-write-new-tests shape of medium-coding and long-agentic. Sized for context-window pressure: ~24KB of source + accumulated tool reads should trigger compaction. Sibling workloads: #445 (performance-investigation), #446 (cli-from-scratch). Closes #444.

workload.description: Consolidate three duplicated validators (email, phone, url) across user_service, account_service, billing_service into a single canonical `src/validators.py`. Three variants per validator x three services = 9 duplicated function bodies to dedup. Keep all 81 existing tests passing. Multi-file edit shape; design judgment about wrapper vs re-export; targets context-window pressure for compaction.
workload.prompt:
CROSS-FILE REFACTOR — consolidate duplicated validators

Three services in this project each have their own copies of THREE validators: `validate_email`, `validate_phone`, and `validate_url`. Each validator was implemented independently in each service, and the three implementations of each have drifted into stylistic variants (regex / partition / string-methods) of the same behavior.

Service files (each has all three validators duplicated):

- ${SANDBOX_DIR}/src/user_service.py — regex-based variants
- ${SANDBOX_DIR}/src/account_service.py — partition/split-based variants
- ${SANDBOX_DIR}/src/billing_service.py — string-method based variants

Each service has a small number of consumer functions (e.g., create_user, create_account, add_payment_method) that call the local validators.

Existing tests live under ${SANDBOX_DIR}/tests/ and exercise the validators directly + through the consumer functions. There are 81 tests total across the three test files.

## Your task

Consolidate the duplicated validator implementations into a single canonical version at `${SANDBOX_DIR}/src/validators.py`. The canonical module should define `validate_email`, `validate_phone`, and `validate_url` — one definition each. Update each service so they all go through the shared validators. Don't break any existing tests.

When you're done, the project should have one definition per validator, three services that delegate to them, and all 81 existing tests passing.

## Constraints

- ONE canonical definition per validator at `src/validators.py`.
- All three services use them (import + call).
- Existing tests under tests/ continue to pass — do NOT modify the test files.
- The tests do `from src.<service> import validate_email, validate_phone, validate_url`, so each service must continue to expose those three names at module level (re-export from validators is fine).
- Don't change the OTHER function signatures the services expose (create_user, update_user_website, create_account, update_account_callback, add_payment_method, set_invoice_webhook); those are called by other parts of the codebase.
- Don't over-engineer — three module-level functions are fine; a class hierarchy is not.
- Iterate by running individual test files:
  cd ${SANDBOX_DIR} && python3 -m unittest tests.test_user_service
- Final verification:
  cd ${SANDBOX_DIR} && python3 -m unittest discover -s tests -p 'test_*.py'

## When you're done

You're done when:
1. `src/validators.py` exists with three canonical validator definitions.
2. All three services import + use them (no duplicated validator bodies).
3. The final discover run shows OK with all 81 tests passing.

Reply with a tight summary:
- Where the canonical validators live.
- How the services delegate (re-export vs thin wrapper) and why.
- Test result: the OK / FAILED line from your final discover run.
- One sentence on a design choice you made.
